body:
> CI says there is something slightly off with the stream:
> 
> ```
>   ● Profiling Plugin › should handle output path with folder creation
> 
>     Error [ERR_STREAM_PUSH_AFTER_EOF]: stream.push() after EOF
> 
> 
> 
>       at Tracer._pushString (node_modules/chrome-trace-event/dist/trace-event.js:101:14)
>       at node_modules/chrome-trace-event/dist/trace-event.js:147:22
>       at Tracer.end (node_modules/chrome-trace-event/dist/trace-event.js:118:37)
>       at r (lib/debug/ProfilingPlugin.js:3912:24)
>       at WriteStream.fsStream.on (lib/debug/ProfilingPlugin.js:3456:9)
>       at node_modules/graceful-fs/graceful-fs.js:61:14
> ```

I believe this some inconsistency with streams in older versions of node. Seems to work consistently on more recent versions of node. Instead of debugging this difference, perhaps we can just merge this into the webpack@6 branch and let the change roll out with that?
author: markjm

body:
> I believe this some inconsistency with streams in older versions of node. Seems to work consistently on more recent versions of node. Instead of debugging this difference, perhaps we can just merge this into the webpack@6 branch and let the change roll out with that?

I would prefer to minimize the differences between webpack 5 and 6, so this seem to be solve-able in webpack 5 too. If not check node.js version and only enable it in compatible versions
author: sokra

body: I dont think typescript has any way of understanding class method overloads with jsdoc ([it's kinda janky anyways](https://github.com/jsdoc/jsdoc/issues/1017)), so i just made the arguments optional and left documentation. body:
> SyntaxError: Cannot use 'import.meta' outside a module

I think it is lack supported `import.meta` in tests, because we use `vm.runInThisContext`, we can:
- Skip test
- Refactor our tests and support it
author: alexander-akait

body: Run `node_modules/.bin/jest test/TestCasesNormal.basictest.js`
author: alexander-akait

body:
> * Skip test

`it.skip()` would not work here as it's issue with parsing the module. Any other ideas ?

> * Refactor our tests and support it

I guess that something `require` individual test files ?
To be able to test ES6 modules, we would need to dynamic `import` it instead.
That should be ok even in `vm.runInThisContext` I think.

And that only for tests which are for the es6 module target, because things like `__filename` don't work in es6 module, so we could not switch all tests.

Could you advise what enumerates the test files?
Is that `jest` ?
https://jestjs.io/docs/ecmascript-modules
author: pavelsavara

author: alexander-akait

body: @alexander-akait I have exposed some static methods to test for the plugin, and please review it.
author: aleen42

body:
These numbers are not intended to be replaced. This happens when the hash value is identical to the module id, which will lead to weird results...
If you want to make sure to avoid that, we probably want to only generate hashes that contain at least 1 non-numeric char.
I could think of a smarter slice in `hash.digest().slice(0, hashLength)`. We could implement a smart hash slice that avoids only-numeric hashes e. g. by offsetting the hash when it matches `/^\d+n?$/` (also avoiding bigint `n` suffix).
author: sokra

body: @sokra @alexander-akait, it seems like a rare situation. To build up such a test case, I need to use 1 bit of hash, like `[contenthash:1].js`, to generate a more common situation to reproduce it. Besides, it is better to use `acorn` to parse and replace hashes within JavaScript assets, rather than using loose regressions. Besides, metacharacters (`\b`) should be a better choice during replacement.

body:
@ScriptedAlchemy well MF internally will change a bit, and thats what i would need to discuss with you on deep to confirm all user cases and from there write the correct documentation

but, with this way MF can works fully sync because we are taking the premise that shared libraries are globals and remotes are being loaded as promises that are easy to manage at high level code like react with react.lazy and react.suspense

the second point is  that a MF library loaded as child into a host, MUST have direct access to the data exposes from the host, for the moment im making this available via shared library also but probably we can improve this more, and in remotes i added a way to import parent details instead be downloading this like we was talking before

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/86263126/151655382-01adc9a5-191e-4afd-b69f-e059f7d62518.png)
author: crodriguez-plitzi

body:
@ScriptedAlchemy @sokra this change is not just for MF, with the change done we can finally solve the problem about duplicated dependencies like react and the popular error message, because when we share libraries like react, they will be available globally  as singleton access

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15271/files#diff-c3b1610b00ec187ec39f60ef90682220aef7392d2943458cc005d123cbcd8dd9R89

@alexander-akait Rename the exportion to 'modulesNames' because i saw there's a internal modules named and it break some other tests.

sokra

body:
@sokra Thanks for the answear Tobias.
Yeah i know and in fact i am doing this right now.
But there are kind of 7/8 modules and this is only 1 project, there are a couple of other at UFMS, this is part of a project from a Master Degree here in brazil.
To bring you a little of context: this is to help "third world" project who used a lot of offline PWA/SPA because of our crappy connections so we cache stuff that are separate in federate modules.

About the bundle size, its only few lines with export an array, but if i can improve it to dont be so relevant i will happly do.
Thanks in advance
author: schirrel

body:
I still don't see a reason to add this feature. You can do that in userland and that give very high flexibility.
Adding it as webpack feature, would require maintaining it. And then somebody requests to rename `moduleNames`, or somebody requests to change the name since they have a module named `moduleNames`. The next one like to exclude some module names. The two lines in the configuration are just a better and easier to understand solution.
author: sokra

body:
Yo Tobias that data application trick is amazing. I’m going to try that! We can always make a another plugin that composes this onto module federation. No need to alter webpack for this data. 

One thing I think could be really helpful - can webpack emit an optional chunkMap. This would be very useful for client/server correlation. 

If commonjs remotes could call remote.ChunkMap. Where I include some stats about the client build

body:
> Also, not really a bug, but just exploring this without a doc
> 
> I found that the default mode is `sync` and so if using `chunkName` without a `mode` it will have no effect, maybe we can change the default to 'lazy-once' if a chunkName is given? Otherwise I'd add a compilation warning if chunkName is given with a mode that doesn't use the chunkName
> 
> ```
> const vuetify = import.meta.webpackContext('vuetify/lib/locale', {
>   recursive: false,
>   chunkName: 'locales', // This does nothing until we add mode: 'lazy' or mode: 'lazy-once'
>   regExp: /(en|hu)\.js$/i,
> });
> ```

yeah, need to add a warning if not `lazy*` mode

author: vankop

author: aleen42

body:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/releases/tag/v5.69.0

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15289
author: vankop

body: @vankop I have met another problem, even if the content hash could not be numeric only. For example, I have some numeric literals minified by the `terser` plugin into a decimal exponent notation like `31536e6`. If there is another chunk whose content hash is exactly `536e6`, the problem arises again.
author: aleen42

body: This is the known problem, please use longer hash
author: alexander-akait

body: @alexander-akait why not using JS parser to replace exactly string literals?
author: aleen42

body: @aleen42 Because it is bad for performance, very
author: alexander-akait

body: ## [CodSpeed Performance Report](https://codspeed.io/webpack/webpack/branches/aleen42%3ArealContentHashPlugin) ### Merging #15453 will **degrade performances by 73.23%** Comparing aleen42:realContentHashPlugin (e1e7547) with main (24b1ec7) ### Summary `⚡ 4` improvements `❌ 1` regressions `✅ 128` untouched benchmarks > :warning: _Please fix the performance issues or [acknowledge them on CodSpeed](https://codspeed.io/webpack/webpack/branches/aleen42%3ArealContentHashPlugin)._ ### Benchmarks breakdown | | Benchmark | `BASE` | `HEAD` | Change | | --- | --------- | ----------------------- | ------------------- | ------ | | ⚡ | `` benchmark "devtool-eval-source-map", scenario '{"name":"mode-development-rebuild","mode":"development","watch":true}' `` | 41.9 ms | 11.8 ms | ×3.6 | | ❌ | `` benchmark "devtool-source-map", scenario '{"name":"mode-development-rebuild","mode":"development","watch":true}' `` | 11.7 ms | 43.6 ms | -73.23% | | ⚡ | `` benchmark "future-defaults", scenario '{"name":"mode-development-rebuild","mode":"development","watch":true}' `` | 52.6 ms | 47.1 ms | +11.71% | | ⚡ | `` benchmark "lodash", scenario '{"name":"mode-development-rebuild","mode":"development","watch":true}' `` | 45.7 ms | 38 ms | +20.17% | | ⚡ | `` benchmark "many-chunks-esm", scenario '{"name":"mode-development-rebuild","mode":"development","watch":true}' `` | 61.9 ms | 9.8 ms | ×6.3 |
author: codspeed-hq

body:
However, for most developers, the plugin has broken source content, which may lead to an exception, which is unacceptable.

The only resolution is to mark the accurate positions where hashes will appear in the source content and replace them according to their positions.

I temporarily disable the plugin via `optimization.realContentHash`.
author: aleen42

body: @aleen42 Why don't use longer hashes?
author: alexander-akait

body: @alexander-akait A longer hash still cannot ensure that it won't be conflict with source contents, even if it is a lower chance. Furthermore, we have no techunism to detect the unexpected replacements.
author: aleen42

body:
> @alexander-akait A longer hash still cannot ensure that it won't be conflict with source contents, even if it is a lower chance. Furthermore, we have no techunism to detect the unexpected replacements.

But the chance is extremely small, with the same chance you can get a hash collision, and it will not work too
author: alexander-akait

body: @alexander-akait what do you think about this PR? should we somehow define unique name in this case?
author: vankop

body: @vankop I think validation of the `uniqueName` make sense too, because using the same can be problems too, for better dx let's generate a new unique name (just add a number based on index if multicompiler array) and output a warning
author: alexander-akait

body: @alexander-akait fixed. As I understand cache does not work correctly with same `name`, so `name` always should be unique
author: vankop

body: fixed @alexander-akait
author: vankop

body:
@vankop I made some improvements of your idea
- avoid extra loops (better perf), we already apply webpack default options, so for multi compiler mode we can just pass index of compiler and make default name using this index
- by default we use `name` and `mode` for cache names, so let's respect if for multi compiler mode (i.e. when both configuration doesn't have name, we use `name + mode + compiler_index`)
- now we output the warning if you have `name: "default"` for both compilers (test cases added)
- if you have node API for webpack you don't need to apply new default, we will do it under the hood (better DX)
author: alexander-akait

body: Also always output warnings when you explicitly set the same cache names
author: alexander-akait

body:
I've been avoiding an [upgrade for node-fetch](https://github.com/chromaui/chromatic-cli/pull/559) because they import `node:http` nowadays, but since we [bundle everything up](https://github.com/chromaui/chromatic-cli/blob/e57a2ccf1d6cb87efe800ce1749fa870e680b8ed/webpack-action.config.js) to support Node 12, it fails.

```
Error: Cannot find module 'node:http'
```

Will this fix resolve the problem? I'd appreciate some pointers on the proper Webpack config to support Node 12 and up.

See also https://github.com/node-fetch/node-fetch/issues/1367#issuecomment-1024321584

body: @sokra I applied this patch to the original issue described in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15541 What I observed: - In the [reproduction repo](https://github.com/tmeasday/storybook-lazy-compilation-webpack/tree/main) (where we trigger a bug via hitting multiple route w/ playwright) it didn't really make much difference. - In an actual larger app, where you can trigger the issue by quickly changing "routes" (components in storybook), the behaviour changed (probably you'd say improved) but didn't resolve: - Previously ([on the `lazy-compilation` branch](https://github.com/storybookjs/design-system/tree/lazy-compilation), if you change routes quickly (run `yarn storybook`, then in the browser, use the `cmd-down` shortcut repeatedly to jump between components), you would eventually see a 404 which bubbles up as an error from `import()`: - With the patch applied ([on the `lazy-compilation-webpack-15586` branch](https://github.com/storybookjs/design-system/tree/lazy-compilation-webpack-15586)), the same action leads to eventually the `import()` just hanging, and this error appearing in the console:
author: tmeasday

<w> Build dependencies behind this expression are ignored and might cause incorrect cache invalidation.
`

> You should put @types/eslint in your deps

Why? that makes no sense. I'm not developing with eslint in the project where I found this error.

body: But you use types from `@types/eslint`
author: alexander-akait

body:
`eslint-scope` doesn't used in `webpack/types.d.ts`, so `@types/eslint-scope` can be moved to `devDependencies`.
But `estree` used in `webpack/types.d.ts`, so `@types/estree` should remain in `dependencies`.
author: Semigradsky

body: both estree and eslint-scope are used ( estree as types inside webpack code base, eslint-scope https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/ConcatenatedModule.js#L8 )
author: vankop

body: `eslint-scope` used, but only as `devDependency`. There are no `eslint-scope` 's types exports.
author: Semigradsky

body:
@Semigradsky is correct! As a rule of thumb, the only types which should be in `dependencies` are types which are re-exposed as part of *your* public API. Types which are part of your implementation details can be in `devDependencies`, as they are not exposed to end users.

One way of making that intuition concrete is: is the type in your published `.d.ts` *and* are you getting types via the `@types` package for it? Then the `@types` package needs to be in your `dependencies` or your `peerDependencies`.
author: chriskrycho

body:
> Let's add test for `@media` inside `@supports` and versa vice

I added more test cases as you asked.

> also I think we should handle them in `@layer` and `@namespace` (legacy) at-rules

I did not implemented anything with `@namespace` because [it's not supported right now](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/css/CssParser.js#L346).

I started to implement the support of `@layer` but I encountered issues with `@import` which does not implement [conditional rules](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/@import#importing_css_rules_conditionally).
It is necessary to fix this so [importing CSS rules into a cascade layer](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/@import#importing_css_rules_into_a_cascade_layer) would be done the same way.
So, in my opinion, it would be better to handle `@import` fixes and `@layer` support this in another PR.
author: noreiller

body: not sure what you expect.. if you want webpack ignore `url(...)` we should make webpack magic comment `/* webpackIgnore: true */` to work in css..
author: vankop

body:
Leading slash urls in CSS are a standard and adding a webpack magic comment is not the best way to handle that.
As `url(file:///img)` would work as a default with webpack, I would recommend to follow the standard way.
author: noreiller

body:
I mean webpack will process `/url` anyway. Maybe right now it is wrong in resolving ( it should throw error aka `"url 'blabla' wasn't resolved"` ), but webpack should handle url.
_______

If you want to ignore it you should use `webpackIgnore: true`
author: vankop

body:
Webpack CSS support cannot require to add this magic comment anywhere an absolute url is used. I'm sorry but it's only a hack whereas you a support means respect the standard.
I can use the comment as a temporary fix but it's not a long term solution.
author: noreiller
vankop

body:
Yeah, I get your point. From my point of view, the code & standard one, I would assume that webpack will follow the standard before trying to bundle everything.
Anyway, I can understand that having different behavior between JS and CSS url usage wouldn't be the best solution.

CSS supports is an awesome feature and for the context, I'm trying to enable it on a big Next.js app.
author: noreiller

body: If you want something not be bundled you would usually flag it as "external" with the `externals` option.
author: sokra

body:
> If you want something not be bundled you would usually flag it as "external" with the `externals` option.

Thanks, I managed to use it with the `asset` externalType, which is not (yet?) documented on https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/#externalstype.
In my case, I can create an `externals` function to check if the issuer is a `CSS` file and the url starts with the listed assets paths. 
As previously said, it would be really nice if webpack can adopt the point of view of the CSS standard.

body:
Opting out of side effects also opts out of evaluation of modules in source order. So technically we are allowed to place side effect free modules in any order, but at least it must be deterministic. Without module concatenation modules are evaluated in usage order, so sorting by range start seems correct.

Test case need to test the `sideEffects: false` case.

body: Opening as ready for review to get initial feedback. 🙂 I contacted my company's CLA management team to work out the EasyCLA.
author: gluxon

body: I believe I saw the same bottleneck before, then reported as https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15231.
author: JoostK

body:
@JoostK Thanks for linking that. I was curious if others had seen this issue. I would be interested to know if the patch here alleviates the problem for your team if you have a moment to manually apply and test.

While this PR brought our team's `merge` time down from 1 minute, I did notice times still vary from 1s to 4s. I think `Snapshot` can non-deterministically end up in a deeply nested state that takes `SnapshotIterator` a bit of time to flatten. I was hoping to continue investigating there, but I'm not sure if it's worthwhile yet unless it would help others.
author: gluxon

body: I just checked with this patch applied and it didn't _seem_ any faster. I haven't taken measurements but it doesn't seem like it resolves #15231.
author: JoostK

body:
Got it. I'd wager there's more work needed here too. The next step I was thinking of was attempting to serialize the `Snapshot` tree and comparing it between the fast ~1s `merge` case and slow ~4s case.

As a heads up, the `watch` -> `merge` slowness this PR addresses can be difficult to perceive without measuring/profiling. The watch call happens after Webpack says it's ready/done, so the timings here aren't typically printed on the console. If you have time, I would be curious if the `lib/Watching.js` timings are different in @mattlewis92's profiling here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15231#issue-1111649057
author: gluxon

body:
> * Does this change make sense, or should I investigate improving performance of this `watch` call in a different way?

Makes sense to me

> * Would it make sense to use [`WeakRef`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/WeakRef) to prevent these saved iterable objects from living longer than necessary? I think we'd have to check for the API's existence and gracefully fallback to support older Node.js versions.

I don't think that makes sense, since each iterator only keeps a referenced to the Snapshot, so it doesn't add any large memory overhead. Actually it reduces memory since only a single iterator is created per Snapshot.

> Items iterated during merge: 1198677, 23045.103875994682 ms

> Items iterated during merge: 464918, 1726.2539209723473 ms

It's interesting that reducing the count by 50% improves performance by 13x. Why is that? Maybe the SnapshotIterables are far more expensive than the remaining Iterables during merge, and the number of SnapshotIterables was also reduced by ~13x.

> I think `Snapshot` can non-deterministically end up in a deeply nested state

The way `Snapshot`s are optimized is really non-deterministic, since it depends a lot on the order and speed in creating them (which depends on fs operations).

> I contacted my company's CLA management team to work out the EasyCLA.

:+1:

The PR looks good and I'll merge it afterwards
author: sokra

body:
> I don't think that makes sense, since each iterator only keeps a referenced to the Snapshot, so it doesn't add any large memory overhead. Actually it reduces memory since only a single iterator is created per Snapshot.

Nice. Glad to have a second pair of eyes to confirm that.

> It's interesting that reducing the count by 50% improves performance by 13x. Why is that? Maybe the SnapshotIterables are far more expensive than the remaining Iterables during merge, and the number of SnapshotIterables was also reduced by ~13x.

I was surprised by this too and came to the same conclusion as you. I think the `SnapshotIterable` objects that were duplicated were typically more nested/larger than the more unique objects.

I hadn't thought of this until your memory comment, but I'd expect garbage collection of `SnapshotIterable` objects to happen less in this `watch` -> `merge` call now. There may have been performance improvements from just not running GC as frequently.

> The way Snapshots are optimized is really non-deterministic, since it depends a lot on the order and speed in creating them (which depends on fs operations).

👍 Thanks for confirming that. That's nice to know.
author: gluxon

author: alexluongfm

body:
> Was there any documentation on how to use ignoreBrowserWarnings? I can see [ignoreWarnings](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#ignorewarnings) on the main site only.

Is in progress, but it's a boolean in the output section
https://github.com/webpack/webpack.js.org/pull/6785
author: indeediansbrett

body: Not sure whether `ignoreBrowserWarnings` is a good naming, since module federation also works in other target like node.js
author: ahabhgk

body: @ahabhgk Any ideas for a better name? We can do it using soft deprecation
author: alexander-akait

body:
Currently I don't have a specific name, only some ideas:

ignoreRuntimeWarnings? ignoreConsoleWarnings? ignoreTargetWarnings? or something else that make ignoreXxxWarnings target-agnostic

or just `output.ignoreWarnings`? but users may confuse with the top-level ignoreWarnings.
author: ahabhgk

body:
@ahabhgk I am fine with another name, we can make a soft deprecation (just a message) or we can just add `TODO` for webpack@6

Maybe even `ignoreModuleFederationsWarnings`
author: alexander-akait

/*

author: bpapez

body:
Good find @Jack-Works !

From an exploitability perspective, does this only affect build process / machine / runner? My interpretation of the above is that an attacker would have to submit malicious input into the repo / CI/CD and it would only get executed at build time, on the build machine / runner. After webpack runs, the remaining static js bundles would not include any malicious code, right?

I noticed that your CVE was scored with network attack vector in NVD. https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-28154
Can you help us understand where an attacker needs to be to exploit this? Did you select network because an attacker can submit code to github? The exploit would only be triggered by existing build process on the build machine / runner, right?
author: devd88

body:
> Good find @Jack-Works !
> 
> From an exploitability perspective, does this only affect build process / machine / runner? My interpretation of the above is that an attacker would have to submit malicious input into the repo / CI/CD and it would only get executed at build time, on the build machine / runner. After webpack runs, the remaining static js bundles would not include any malicious code, right?

This attack requires the code compiled is already malicious (therefore the output may not be clean already). Yes, it only attacks the builder, but the developer's computer is also a builder.

> Did you select network because an attacker can submit code to github? The exploit would only be triggered by existing build process on the build machine / runner, right?

Yes. Yes.
author: Jack-Works

body:
any plans for this?
Angular 15.1.1 (and possible older versions) giving this with "npx check-peer-dependencies"
```
  ✅  ajv ^8.0.0 is required by ajv-formats@2.1.1 (8.12.0 is installed)
  ✅  ajv ^8.8.2 is required by ajv-keywords@5.1.0 (8.12.0 is installed)
  ❌  ajv ^6.9.1 is required by ajv-keywords@3.5.2) (8.12.0 is installed)
  ❌  ajv ^6.9.1 is required by ajv-keywords@3.5.2) (8.12.0 is installed)
```

"yarn why ajv" gives:
- ajv@8.x comes as dependency of angular itself ("@angular-devkit#build-angular#@angular-devkit#core#ajv")
- ajv@6.x comes via webpack/schema-utils (@angular-devkit#build-angular#webpack#schema-utils#ajv)

schema-utils 4.0.0 will have dependencies directing ajv@8 and so dependencies within angular 15 would be ok again

(Actually I don't see any problems within my application, but I like a clean build ;-) )

body:
Hi! @alexander-akait do you have any clue about the following test failure? Thanks!

- `init?.();` `SyntaxError: Unexpected token .` on Node 10

I emit code like

```js
runtimeTemplate.supportsOptionalChaining() ? "init?.();" : "if (init) init();",
```

So I'm not sure why Node 10 test runner will emit optional chaining.

```
● TestCases › cache pack › parsing › harmony-export-import-specifier › harmony-export-import-specifier should pre-compile to fill disk cache (2nd)
InfrastructureLogs while compiling:

    message = Pack got invalid because of write to: Compilation/codeGeneration|/home/runner/work/webp...
```

I wonder is there any guide of touching the cache (I believe it's about `serialize` and `deserialize`)
author: Jack-Works
nicolo-ribaudo

body:
```
● TestCases › cache pack › parsing › harmony-export-import-specifier › harmony-export-import-specifier should pre-compile to fill disk cache (2nd)
InfrastructureLogs while compiling:

    message = Pack got invalid because of write to: Compilation/codeGeneration|/home/runner/work/webp...
```

Ok I figure out this is caused by the using of the magic comment `import /* webpackDefer: true */ * as deferred from './deferred.js'`. If I use the native syntax `import defer *`, this is not an issue, but I'm not sure why it *is* an issue
author: Jack-Works

body:
I have updated the doc, added the following content:

I suggest putting the magic comment after the `from` keyword, which is compatible with TypeScript, Babel, SWC, and Flow if the necessary option to preserve comments is enabled.

Please do not put it after the "import" keyword (like `import /* webpackDefer: true */ * as ns from './mod.mjs'`), the filesystem cache is broken, and I don't know why.

ESBuild is not compatible, please read https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/1439 and https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/309. Esbuild may support this feature ad-hoc like other webpack magic comments.
author: Jack-Works

body:
@nicolo-ribaudo 

> I see that acorn-import-defer is an optional peer dependency. When Webpack implemented import attributes, acorn-import-assertions was added as a normal dependency, so that the syntax would be available to all users. Should we do something similar here?

Yes we need to move it to dependencies 

> Should we support the comment, even now that we support the syntax? Prettier already supports this syntax, and it will hopefully be included in the next minor release of TypeScript.

I think it's a question of syntax, if we are sure that it won't change we can avoid comment syntax (hope we will not repeat `assert`/`with` history - import attributes, when it was renamed after long discussions), on the other hand some tools may take more time to implement this, for example es;int, which will make problems...

Anyway it is experiment, so we can introduce comment syntax later 

> Does webpack have a way to run test262 tests? There are a bunch of them for import defer at https://github.com/tc39/test262/tree/main/test/language/import/import-defer

Good question, we have some parts of tests, but not all, I'll mark this as a task for myself
author: alexander-akait

body: @Jack-Works Thank you for this great work, can you make rebase, I think we will merge it for the next minor release
author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body:
> @Jack-Works
> 
> > Please do not put it after the "import" keyword (like import /* webpackDefer: true */ * as ns from './mod.mjs'), the filesystem cache is broken, and I don't know why.
> 
> Can you provide feedback on this? Maybe we miss something to serialize? What error do you have?

If the comment is put after `import`, the cache created from `.a` is not hit in `.b` ("Pack got invalid because of write to ..."). As I can recall, the hash of the module is changed.
author: Jack-Works

body: It seems I'm not doing it the right way, it needs to know the resourcePath when creating the parser, might need some suggestion.
author: ahabhgk

body: @ahabhgk Added couple tests, maybe we should use it as a default `css` type, so you don't need to set `type: "css/auto"`, but let's consider it for another PR(s)
author: alexander-akait

body:
> maybe we should use it as a default `css` type, so you don't need to set `type: "css/auto"`, but let's consider it for another PR(s)

Use it as a default `css` sounds good to me, and we can add something like [`auto`](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader#auto) on parserOptions to control this behavior
author: ahabhgk

body: @ahabhgk No, we will have `css` and `css/auto` and other, because you want to avoid handling `.modules.` as modules, I mean by default use `css/auto` instead `css`, but we need to think about it
author: alexander-akait

body: Ohh, got it
author: ahabhgk

body: @TheLarkInn I tried hard to reproduce it in the demo, and then wrote test cases, but in the end I couldn't reproduce it. I think the [Inner-module tree-shaking](https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#inner-module-tree-shaking) part of the code is complicated 😢 ......

author: alexander-akait

body:
> @alexander-akait This problem occurred in a project of our company, So I can't upload this part of the reproduced code 😢 ，I've been trying to reproduce in a simple demo, but it always works 😅
> 
> What I found out is that a `Module（29971）` has a runtimes array（RuntimeSpec[]）
> 
> ```js
> {
>   29971: function (t, e, r) {
>     
>     e.ZP =
>       5224 == r.j
>         ? function (t, e, r) {
>             var i,
>               o = e.payload.cacheKey;
>             return n(n({}, t), (((i = {})[o] = m(t[o], e, r)), i));
>           }
>         : null;
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> ```js
> /** @typedef {string | SortableSet<string> | undefined} RuntimeSpec */
> ```
> 
> The value of this runtimes is `[new SortableSet([5224, 5491, 1803]), new SortableSet([9768, 7327, 7069])]`
> 
> But here only `runtimes[0]` is used when generating [runtimeConditionExpression](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/dependencies/PureExpressionDependency.js#L95), the generated code is as follows
> 
> ```js
> e.ZP = 5224 == r.j ? xxx : null
> ```
> 
> At this time, if `9768` in `runtimes[1]` also depends on this `Module（29971`）, The export of `Module（29971）`is null ❌
> 
> So I changed the parameter passed in when generating [runtimeConditionExpression](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/dependencies/PureExpressionDependency.js#L95) from runtime to _**mergedRuntimes**_
> 
> ```diff
> -			const runtimeCondition = filterRuntime(runtime, runtime => {
> +			const merged = deepMergeRuntime(runtimes, runtime);
> +			const runtimeCondition = filterRuntime(merged, runtime => {
> ```
> 
> The code generated by `Module (29971)` becomes the following, My problem has also been solved ✅, because the js of `9768 Entrypoint` can also get the export of `Module (29971)`
> 
> ```js
> e.ZP = [5224, 9768].includes(r.j) ? xxx : null
> ```
> 
> ![](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23253540/217249116-87bb19e1-e1e5-492c-a383-cff431450d10.png)

@alexander-akait I'm not sure what conditions  a Module has a runtimes array (RuntimeSpec[]). So far, no one else has reported this problem, and I have begun to wonder if this project uses a special plugin 🤷‍♂️. I can't provide more information at the moment, so let's put this pr on hold for now ... 
❤️ Thanks for following up so far
author: xiaoxiaojx

body:
I just stumbled upon this issue after finding @xiaoxiaojx 's [blog post](https://zhuanlan.zhihu.com/p/604359536) pointing here (thank you!). We started seeing the mismatched module ID/null function export after switching from `runtimeChunk: 'single'` to `runtimeChunk: true` in a large multi-entrypoint repo under latest webpack 5.89.0.

The workaround to disable `optimization.innerGraph` seems to work, but unfortunately increases bundle sizes by quite a lot per-entrypoint (presumably due to benefits we'd otherwise gain from this optimisation). I'd rather not do that, so I'm wondering what the current status of this PR is? 

I see there's some talk about tests, but my guess is that it might be hard to come up with a robust test here that captures the behaviour, since in our case we only saw this for one specific module in our monorepo out of thousands, and only after shipping a second, seemingly benign change (which presumably affected chunking/module concatenation/something else under the hood).

If I were to take a stab at the conditions that cause this, I'd say:

* `runtimeChunk: true`
* a named module export that's used across two separate entrypoints
* importing and using the named export in the second entrypoint
* ...more variables that are hard to track down

If there's more info I can provide that would help get this merged, that would be great.
author: jdb8

body: One thing that I was unsure of was whether or not a given asset could be expected to have multiple asset hashes in it's filename. To be defensive against that case, I've designed `hashReplace` and `hashMatch` to always iterate through the entire array of regular expressions, but if there's a known invariant that only one can match then we can speed this up even more by breaking on the first match/replacement.
author: ryanwilsonperkin

body: Hey @lvivski thanks for bringing it to my attention. Could you include some information about the format of the hashes that you're replacing and the number and size of assets that you're replacing them in? And are you able to isolate the timeout to this part of the code?
author: ryanwilsonperkin

body:
@ryanwilsonperkin 

> Could you include some information about the format of the hashes

we're using `xxxhash64` 8 chars.

> the number and size of assets that you're replacing them in

~5 runtime assets
~10k hashes to replace. 5k from content hash and 5k through the hook with [`webpack-subresource-integrity`](https://github.com/waysact/webpack-subresource-integrity). I've also tried disabling SRI and it didn't help.

> And are you able to isolate the timeout to this part of the code?

Yes, after creating a custom `webpack` build with this change reverted, it works fine.


I have just ran your stress-test build from https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/webpack-16758

```
CPU: 2.4 GHz 8-Core Intel Core i9
RAM: 64 GB 2667 MHz DDR4
OS: Mac OS 13.2.1
NodeJS: v19.7.0
```

### `v5.76.0`

```
Run 1: 50487 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 51002 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 51650 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
```

### `v5.76.0 w/ changes from this PR reverted`

```
Run 1: 9812 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 10482 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 10761 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
```

Around **5x** decrease in processing time if I revert this change.
author: lvivski

body:
I'm getting very different results than you on that stress test build:

```
CPU: Apple M1 Pro
RAM: 16 GB
OS: Mac OS 13.2.1
NodeJS: v19.4.0
```

## `v5.75.0`
```
Run 1: 46028 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 46286 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 45108 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
```

## `v5.76.0`
```
Run 1: 25504 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 26187 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 27294 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
```

Your machine appears to be more powerful, but it seems odd to me that when running the test from https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/webpack-16758 that your results would be twice as slow against `5.76.0` but four times as fast against `5.75.0`. 

> the number and size of assets that you're replacing them in

I should clarify this further, I meant the size (in bytes) of the assets that you are replacing the hashes in, and how many of those assets you're processing.
author: ryanwilsonperkin

body:
Sorry, I should have clarified that I wasn't running `5.7_6_` against `5.7_5_` on your test setup . I was comparing `5.7_6_` and then manually removing changes from this PR, but keeping all other changes from `5.7_6_`.

I wonder if this is `M1` vs `Intel` difference. Do you use any CI? If so, do you also see similar improvement numbers there?

> I meant the size (in bytes) of the assets that you are replacing the hashes in

This is generally hard to tell, ranging between several Kb to low hundreds, with about 150Mb generated JS total. 

But yeah, I think that running multiple small RexExp's is less efficient than a single big one, in our case, because of the total number and size of the files. While initialization time might be longer for a big regex, subsequent comparisons are faster with only a single pass through the each file/string.

When I was investigating perf for #16491 I wasn't able to create a test setup that would be able to re-create the same level of memory pressure, that our production build has.
author: lvivski

@alexander-akait @TheLarkInn could we test this one more time.. problem here is that if you have small amount of chunks (not a 3000 as in @ryanwilsonperkin example 😀 ) => it is fine to concat them + matching on huge chunk content would be better. So what this changes does in reality, if you have 90kb chunk and 20 refs ( hashes ) => webpack creates 20 regexps and run them 20 times on 90kb chunk. 

---------------------- 

maybe if you have vice versa setup => a lot of small chunks, this give some performance benefits.

----------------------

Also I guess this is very Node.js performance related thing, so it could **really** depends on Node.js version
author: vankop

body:
I've got access to a powerful Intel-based machine through work and can confirm what you're seeing:

```
CPU: Intel Xeon (32) @ 1.999GHz
RAM: ~200Gb
OS: Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS x86_64
Node: v19.7.0
```

## `5.75.0`
```
Run 1: 15341 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 15118 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 16097 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin  
```

## `5.76.0`
```
Run 1: 70324 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 2: 67705 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
Run 3: 70182 ms asset processing > RealContentHashPlugin
```

So perhaps this comes down to architecture set or processor speed. I don't think that `webpack` should focus on optimizing for M1s at the expense of other architectures since they're more likely to be the ones performing these large builds, so I'd agree that this should be considered a regression and reverted.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16803

body:
I see the point @TheLarkInn is making. I guess before going forward in any direction, I would like to understand consensus of what we want `NodeTargetPlugin` to actually do - specify _latest_ Node.js builtins as externals regardless of what version webpack is running on? or specify the _current_ Node.js builtins as externals?

If we're specifying latest Node builtins as externals, what is supposed to happen in the examples like `diagnostics_channel`? Or when you're building for Node<15 but webpack considers `util/types` an external when it doesn't actually exist?

Another can of worms that's opened up by this is that webpack allows you to specify which node version you're targeting with `nodeX.Y` as the `target` but it's not explicitly specified what `node` itself means - does it mean the latest Node version or the current? Regardless of which direction we go in, I think that should be clarified.

If possible/desired, we could also rely on the `is-core-module` package and pass it the explicit version specified in `target`. That package defaults to the current Node version if that makes any difference.

As for the CI route, my concern would be that since CI runs on multiple Node versions, different runs would produce different results. Presumably you would have to run this particular check on only the latest Node version, which goes back to the question at the beginning.
author: vetruvet

body:
@vetruvet 

> specify latest Node.js builtins as externals regardless of what version webpack is running on

This :+1: 

> If we're specifying latest Node builtins as externals, what is supposed to happen in the examples like diagnostics_channel? Or when you're building for Node<15 but webpack considers util/types an external when it doesn't actually exist?

I think it is out of scope webpack, the same will be without webpack, you have to handle such cases on the application level using `try/catch`, webpack just bundle your code, not more

> Another can of worms that's opened up by this is that webpack allows you to specify which node version you're targeting with nodeX.Y as the target but it's not explicitly specified what node itself means - does it mean the latest Node version or the current? Regardless of which direction we go in, I think that should be clarified.

> If possible/desired, we could also rely on the is-core-module package and pass it the explicit version specified in target. That package defaults to the current Node version if that makes any difference.

I think here is the same.
 
But I think there's a potential improvement here that will satisfy both of us. We can use `is-core-module` (if it is small and don't have a lot of deps) and throw a warning on such cases (also we should have an option to enable/disable such behaviour, because it can be anoying), so you will see and catch CI fails  

Feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
> @vetruvet
> 
> > specify latest Node.js builtins as externals regardless of what version webpack is running on
> 
> This +1

Given that, we can't really use `is-core-module` (at least not in its current state), since that uses the current Node.js version if one is not specified, and it has no knowledge of what is the latest Node.js version. If we did use `is-core-module` as it is, webpack would have to know what the latest Node.js version is. While that is simpler than the list currently maintained, it's still manual toil. Thoughts?
author: vetruvet

body:
> While that is simpler than the list currently maintained, it's still manual toil. Thoughts?

Yes, but we do it as part of a pre-release script, just move this list of modules to own file and create a script that will generate list of modules and throw an error if we don't make a release on the latest Node.js version, so we will always have the latest modules and it will not be manual work
author: alexander-akait

body: @vetruvet I'm going to close this PR based on our discussions but would you be willing to contribute the creation of a pre-release script?
author: TheLarkInn

body: That works, thanks. I can't promise any timing but I will try to whip something up. If someone else can get to it sooner, more power to them!
author: vetruvet

body:
Okay, we have problems with:
- `eslint-plugin-jsdoc` (one of a transitive package added the `exports` to `package.json` field in patch version and `eslint-plugin-jsdoc` can't get jsdocs tokens inside file)
- `eslint-plugin-prettier` (they added `engines` in the patch release)
- `lint-staged` changed own deps and one of the deps requires node v14

body:
When using `output.hashFunction: "debug"`, `DebugHash.digest` will prefix the result with `"debug-digest-"`:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/4d2bdfc7c3611ba7ea8cdf285557adad5143e4bf/lib/util/createHash.js#L122-L124

`_getModuleGraphHashBigInt` will convert the digest result `BigInt`:

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/5695e98d05101df142909c01e5cbe07c5e4573f3/lib/ChunkGraph.js#L1564

the convert failed because the digest result prefixed with `"debug-digest-"`

This PR move `"debug-digest-"` inside `Buffer.from`, to avoid the convert failure.

(I'm not using "debug" hashFunction for my project, just using it for debugging what is hashed)

body:
Notes for me:
- [x] implement it for `ImportMetaContextDependecyParserPlugin.js`
- [x] use mapping/function style (becaue ESM `import(...)` doesn't support it and we can't implement this for ESM)
- [x] ~~test with externals~~ our externals don't support prefetch/preload, so I will not implement `fetchPriority` too, but it is a potential improvement, but only for `externalsType: "script"`
- [x] `webpackFetchPriority: false`
- [x] less size of runtime generation
- [x] more tests to cover all cases
author: alexander-akait

body:
One comment on @alexander-akait's changes: Now the fetch priority is set per chunk at compile time. From what I see in the code, the chunk will pick a random priority (based on execution order of the code in ChunkPrefetchPreloadPlugin.js) from any of the ChunkGroups it belongs to.
Is my understanding correct here?
author: aboktor

body:
> One comment on @alexander-akait's changes: Now the fetch priority is set per chunk at compile time. From what I see in the code, the chunk will pick a random priority (based on execution order of the code in ChunkPrefetchPreloadPlugin.js) from any of the ChunkGroups it belongs to.
> Is my understanding correct here?

~~It is not random, I just move all runtime code to own function and create an object like {  "chunkId": "featchPriority" }, so we don't have extra runtime~~ I see what do you mean, we should use the `featchPriority` property from the first loaded async chunk (i.e. apply order), because another `featchPriority` doesn't make sense, chunk already loaded
author: alexander-akait

body:
This might have potential issue for exporting value

for example:

```js
// index.js
import common from './common.js'

console.log('common: ', common)

export default common; // <---- add this line
```

```js
// common.js
export default true;
```

and add `output.libraryTarget: "module"`, when another file import the default as lib, lib will be `undefined`

```js
import lib from './dist/main.mjs'
console.log(lib) // <---- undefined
```

you can checkout the repro at https://github.com/ahabhgk/webpack-output-module-repro/tree/lib

PS: I'm not sure whether it's appropriate to use `splitChunks` and `libraryTarget` together, just reporting..
author: ahabhgk

@chenxsan Despite believing that most of the documentation I've provided here is contributor specific, I going to have the bot create an issue for documentation because I believe that most people don't know that webpack's parser will evaluate simple expressions for many module / import specific scenarios (as I'm remembering/learning throughout this process).  

If you personally don't find any value in actually documenting this, then you can close the issue.

body: @alexander-akait: I want to hold off on merging this because @bworline shared a case where there might be some TDZ/Variable/Export ordering issues from this PR's generated bundles.
author: TheLarkInn

body:
> @alexander-akait: I want to hold off on merging this because @bworline shared a case where there might be some TDZ/Variable/Export ordering issues from this PR's generated bundles.

In the bundle0.js generated by this new ConfigCases test, I noticed that the concatenated module logic is generating code that is out-of-order.  Essentially, this:

```
console.log(apple);
...
var apple = 45;
```

Admittedly I did some crazy things with import and export including a circular reference, but I'm thinking it should either yell at me, produce correctly ordered code (if possible), or bail out of concatenating.

To be clear this is existing behavior and not something introduced by this PR.
author: bworline

body: @bworline Can you provide a test case where I can look at this? (maybe link on the test in our repo)
author: alexander-akait

body:
> To be clear this is existing behavior and not something introduced by this PR.

I wasn't aware of this detail, however we should probably look into this 😅 (I'll follow up).
author: TheLarkInn

body:
> @bworline Can you provide a test case where I can look at this? (maybe link on the test in our repo)

I don't know of an existing test case, but this PR branch introduces a new test that currently exhibits the behavior.
author: bworline

<!-- identifier: review-outdated -->

I labeled the Pull Request so reviewers will review it again.

@TheLarkInn Please review the new changes.

> In the bundle0.js generated by this new ConfigCases test, I noticed that the concatenated module logic is generating code that is out-of-order. Essentially, this:

I see, it is a new test case
author: alexander-akait

body: @bworline I looked at this PR and found the problem, shorty - circular dependecies and yes, it was not introduced by this PR, so I think we can safely merge it, catching these cases are tricky, maybe we can disable concat modules for circular dependecies, but in other way our logic works correcntly with many other cases (and simple cases with cirucal dependecies) and disabling this can be a problem, so I prefer to merge this as is and open a new issue about it
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Just want to say you can get some options using:
> 
> ```ts
> Compilation["chunkGroups"][0]
> ```
> 
> Not pretty, but allow to solve most of edge cases

Yup absolutely. Today I'm solving it contextually with: 

```ts
          /**
           * TODO: For now we are going to extract the `ChunkGroup` type from the compilation
           * but once this pull request (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17201) lands,
           * then we can remove this and instead import the `ChunkGroup` type from webpack.
           */
          type ChunkGroup = ReturnType<typeof compilation.addChunkInGroup>;
```
author: TheLarkInn

body: Ideally though if it is a type used as an argument/parameter somewhere in a "public API", its own type should be exposed.

author: TheLarkInn

body:
Took a look through this. I want to validate my understanding. Here is how I understand things in their current state:
1. fetchPriority for a chunk is computed at bundle time (my initial PR was computing fetchPriority for a chunk at runtime, based on which async entrypoint requested it). I want to make sure that it is a conscious decision that we want fetchPriority to be decided at bundle time.
2. fetchPriority will be taken from the async entrypoint with the highest prefetchOrder if more than one exists.

Questions:
Is prefetchOrder computed based on the dependency graph and thus makes sense to use here to decide on fetchPriority?

Comments:
I still think that #2 above is a bit counter intuitive. One thing we could do here is to take the highest fetchPriority from all async entrypoints that require a particular chunk ( undefined < "auto" < "low" < "high" ).

LGTM otherwise :).
@alexander-akait
author: aboktor

body:
> Questions:
> Is prefetchOrder computed based on the dependency graph and thus makes sense to use here to decide on fetchPriority?

You can have `prefetchOrder` (and not have, in this case it is just `0`), so it will be used only when `prefetchOrder` exists, to be compatibility with with prefetch 

> I still think that https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/2 above is a bit counter intuitive. One thing we could do here is to take the highest fetchPriority from all async entrypoints that require a particular chunk ( undefined < "auto" < "low" < "high" ).

Yeah, let's add tests too and give me time to think about it (today), we should not increase our runtime when developer doesn't use it, but I see what do you mean

We need to ship it for the next release :smile:
author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body:
So, this MR will report the **error msg of root cause** of Webpack `ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk 456 failed. (error: https://example.com/a.js)`,
Do I understand correctly?

If so, I hope this MR will get merged ASAP. It is a tricky error msg, makes lot of user confused: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69047420/webpack-code-splitting-chunkloaderror-loading-chunk-x-failed-but-the-chunk-e

Thanks for your great work!
author: JuniorTour

body:
> 

Hi @JuniorTour,  unfortunately this PR won't solve your issue, let me explain:
For this chunk loading error there are 3 possibilities:

1. ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk X failed. (**error**: 
2.  ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk X failed. (**timeout**:
3.  ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk X failed. (**missing**:  

Error case is triggered with error event on the script, so it means there was some problem related with networking, the error event won't give you much more info and to correlate with a network error you might need to use PerformanceObserver and it will give you at least the status code.

Timeout case is specific from webpack, a timeout is defined when the script is added to the dom so if neither error or onload are triggered you will see this error

Missing case is the one we are handling on this PR and it is very misleading as it happens when the load event is triggered but the chunk has not being executed, if the load event is triggered from a script it means that the resource has actually being loaded so in reality this means there has been a runtime error executing the script.
The error should be logged in console also but if you are reviewing logs from an APM tool you won't see the real reason on the log and this is the case i will try to solve with this PR.
author: aespinolopez

> I think we should postpone it for webpack v6, cssnano is big and slow and we need to think about it deeply, perhaps we can collaborate with the rspack team

If speed is the concern then we could try out [LightningCSS](https://lightningcss.dev/). `css-minimizer-webpack-plugin` has support for LightningCSS - https://lightningcss.dev/docs.html#with-webpack

Though a different problem with `css-minimizer-webpack-plugin` is Node v10 support.
author: burhanuday

body: @hardfist We should collaborate here, maybe not this exact implementation but have a common goal for CSS minification for native CSS support?
author: TheLarkInn

body:
> @hardfist We should collaborate here, maybe not this exact implementation but have a common goal for CSS minification for native CSS support?

we are using swc-css to minify css because we are also using swc-css to support native css，but swc-css might have bigger size than lightning css，which may be a concern for webpack
if we use lightning-css or swc-css in webpack，should we use the wasm version or napi version？since webpack 5 still sipports node10 then it may have some compatible in old node version

author: alexander-akait

body: For one, I don't feel like handing out my postal address, It feels like a privacy intrusion. I prefer to contribute anonymously but that is certainly not possible here.
author: silverwind

body:
@silverwind I appreciate that you want to keep your privacy.

The main problem for us is that we can only accept the code on the condition that no one claims rights to it. And an email is required in order to link your commit and that you have agreed. If we remove this, then I see no other mechanism for us to make sure that you are you agreed to use your code.

But perhaps theoretically we can implement one time agreement into commit body. Thus, you do not have to go through such a procedure, but only write the required code (maybe one time generated for a PR) in the commit body.

I do not promise that I can solve this problem, because these are legal subtleties, but I will consult and try to find out how we can improve it.
author: alexander-akait

body: @silverwind we understand and appreciate your concern and I have brought this up with the OpenJS Foundation who owns our CLA process. As @alexander-akait I can't promise anything yet except that we will take a concernted effort into investigating it.

body: @timneutkens Yeah, it can help, but I think the valid implementation is WeakCache (i.e. don't validate options which will be validated), we can't just skip it, because developers start to disable it and make errors, also just for intresting - can you compare benches with and without
author: alexander-akait

body:
> but I think the valid implementation is WeakCache

I'd like to avoid this as we're already having memory issues as-is with larger Next.js applications. In case of Next.js we know the configuration for all loaders so the checking is "dead time" in a way. What I found is that on a small application with a single `.css` file it shaves off about 60ms, any additional `.css` file would take another 20ms. In Next.js we already don't pass a schema to getOptions for the loaders that we use internally, only exception is mini-css-extract-plugin which is why it only affects `.css` in Next.js. Alternatively I can vendor the mini-css-extract-plugin code and make the change there, this seemed like the better approach though.
author: timneutkens

body:
@timneutkens I understand this, but ideally solve this problem not only for `Next.js`

> I'd like to avoid this as we're already having memory issues as-is with larger Next.js applications

But you already have options object, so I don't see a lot of memory overhead, we just do not run validation on already validated options

As an alternative solution we can implement https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/465007b40d5d4ed307b7be6c54722a9e7531fb78/lib/NormalModule.js#L561 with an additional argument `skipValidation`, so you can skip it when you know your own options
author: alexander-akait

body:
@timneutkens I think I found the best solutions for everyone:

I did some research about schema-utils perf, if you have a lot of modules we try to validate schema in every loader calls and it takes a lot of time, really:
- We can't cache schema + options, because on every loader call we create a new object with options, maybe we can change this behaviour, but it is tricky and required major release, some bad loaders use it to store different values between different calls, it is dirty way, but developers use it
- But, we can cache compiledSchema https://github.com/webpack/schema-utils/blob/master/src/validate.js#L124, because schema is always the same (i.e.  require("./options.json") in the each loader) we will not spend time on it, we can use WeakCache to allow gc keep memory feels good
- Very often loaders are used without options, so we can skip schema validation for empty objects, but there is required  and some other keywords (anyOf , oneOf  and etc too) and in such cases an empty options object should be failed (because you need to set some required options), our official loaders usually do not use such things, but we can't say the same for other non official loaders, technically we can use this logic - try to compile schema on the first call, if options is empty object cache it and skip in future validation for an empty object and the same schema, but validation empty object is very fast with cache compiled schema (from above) and I don't see any wins here
- When you run a production build you don't need to validate options at all in 99.99%, because you configurate options in the development  mode, you don't need to spend compilation time on validation, so what we can do here - implement API for schema-utils - enableValidation  and disableValidation , these options allow to disable validation at all, so validate(schema, options)  call will do nothing, on webpack side - new  skipValidation option, if true we use disableValidation and disable validation at all, tools like CRA , next.js  and other developers which know your configuration and don't allow to modify them by users can set it to true  and for production and for development mode. Also we have futureDefaults which already speedup build around 20-30%, we can do more and for webpack 6 setup skipValidation: true  by default for production
author: alexander-akait

body:
So shorty:
- New options `skipValidation` (by default `false`)
- `skipValidation: true` we use `schemaUtils.disableValidation()` API and `validate` calls in loaders and plugins do nothing
- `futureDefaults: true` (i.e. default options for webpack v6) will set `skipValidation` to `true` only for production builds, so in future we will not validate options for the production mode at all

You will have two abilities to control validation:
- use `skipValidation`
- use raw `schema-utils` API to enable/disable options validation

I think it should solve most of cases and improve perf for many developers, some companies and developers can use `skipValidation: true` if they don't change configuration or know what they change
author: alexander-akait

body: So you don't need to modify loaders/plugins code
author: alexander-akait

body: Implemented, now we have the `validate` option - https://webpack.js.org/blog/2026-04-08-webpack-5-106/#plugin-validation-with-compilerhooksvalidate, internal plugins uses it, loader too, some plugins from webpack repo still need to update it, we are working on it, soon we will have a major update for all plugins and loaders and will use it
author: alexander-akait

body:
Is there an open issue or a timeframe for the `schema-utils@4` upgrade? I ask because `schema-utils@3` has a production dependency on `ajv-keywords@3` which has a peer dependency on `ajv@6`. Currently we are still using `--legacy-peer-deps` which means we need to install peer dependencies manually, which in turn means we're pinned to `ajv@6`, which is increasingly inconvenient, as the latest, used everywhere else, is `ajv@8`. An upgrade to `schema-utils@4` would be an upgrade to `ajv-keywords@4` or later, which has a peer dependency on `ajv@8` and is completely fine, so this would fix our issue.

We are exploring getting rid of the `--legacy-peer-deps` flag as a separate task, but that's problematic for much nastier reasons which I won't go into. Anyway, any light you can shed on this would be informative. Cheers
author: s100

> Sorry, I don't undestand what you try to solve, please describe it and descrive your problem

I'm trying to add two new parameters that allow end-users to customize the federation behavior when a dependency is shared at the top-level as well as nested under another dependency. Additionally, it allows end-users to specify "preferred" sources when more than one container is providing a shared module. 

Rather than pollute this PR with additional explanations, I will create two separate webpack issues and link it back to this PR. One for exclusion, and One for preferred containers. Does that sound like a good plan ? I will also include stackblitz examples so that the resulting bundle size can be compared with standard module federation. I will address the issues you raised in this PR once we are aligned on the issues at hand.

author: alexander-akait

body:
We forked the webpack plugin and we are going to add this at scale for our 1000+ microfront-ends (or nano-frontends) to be exact. With full control over transitive versions, and sharing behavior, we were able to optimize sharing across many front-ends without paying a heavy price for each front-end. Moving to MF 1.0 from AMD (requirejs) cost us heavily, and this fork will enable us to share more efficiently. 

P.S We tried MF 2.0 with hoisted + externalized runtime, it still is around 5-7x bigger than our forked remote entry. Once we move the semver functions + loadVersionFallback* functions into a shared global plugin in the shell, the size will go down even further. The takeaway for future visitors is that while MF allows each front-end to act as a standalone build, it is detrimental to the web-app's performance. At scale, it is best to delegate certain front-ends to do the "sharing" [provide], and let others consume the shared modules (with major version boundaries of course).
author: tmpaul
alexander-akait

body:
> @tmpaul I am fine to landing it, just want to get some feedback, also can you rebase it
> 
> > We forked the webpack plugin and we are going to add this at scale for our 1000+ microfront-ends (or nano-frontends) to be exact.
> 
> Do you have any other changes in our code expect this? Because I think we can move them to our code (and you no need to use fork anymore or reduce time for maintenancing)

Here are the full list of changes:

1. Add a mechanism to control which versions get shared (i.e registered for sharing as well as consumed from the share scope). The current PR covers this.
2. Do not load fallbacks if the version range that is requested e.g `^4.5.0` is satisfied by a higher version of the fallback as compared to a "loaded" version of the shared module in the runtime. For e.g if `foo` is already loaded at `4.10.0`, and the fallback version is`4.20.0`, **do not** load the fallback. MF 1.5 and MF 2.0 do not have this problem, but we pay a different cost w.r.t the remoteEntry size.
3. Add a way to deduplicate modules with the same versions. It is very common to have the same package + same version at different levels under `node_modules`. MF 1.0 in its current form registers all of these separately, even though the modules are not tree-shaken and therefore effectively the same. This greatly reduces the registration of shared modules that are heavily consumed by other shared modules (e.g themes, icons). It also reduces the build time because fewer fallbacks are built.
4. Ability to build fallbacks only for specific versions and modules. In a ultra large MFE world, we have discovered that having each MFE bring its own copy of a widely shared module is bad for performance, because each MFE author has no idea what another MFE author is going to use. Instead, we ended up creating specialized MFEs that act as the providers of these dependencies, and other MFEs simply consume them. The challenge is that these provider MFEs are typically only consumable by the same major version. e.g if the provider MFE supplies `foo@5.2.0`, and the consumer MFE requires `foo@5.1.0` and `foo@2.1.0` (transitive dependency), we want the `foo@5.1.0` to be shared/consumed, and `foo@2.1.0` to be bundled. Allowing `foo@2.1.0` to be bundled allows us to safely do a "per-module" fallback build. Think of it as a per module `import: false` depending on some condition. Again, this greatly reduces the amount of fallbacks + runtime inconsistencies associated with standard federation sharing.

If these changes are acceptable, I am okay with rebasing and sending an updated PR. Let me know. It is sad that we have zero control of the resolution mechanism used by MF 1.5/2.0 whereas all of the focus is on customizing the share behavior via runtimePlugins. runtimePlugins can achieve most of what I have outlined, but we still pay the cost of the runtime plugin + remote entry + fallback and what not.
author: tmpaul

body: @tmpaul I am fine with it and sounds very reasonable, so I am glad to accept any PRs which improve such cases :+1:

> @tmpaul friendly ping

I am working on rebasing the PR. Not sure if I'll get approval, but I am going to send a new one anyway. Give me a few days to send it
author: tmpaul

body:
> > @tmpaul I am fine to landing it, just want to get some feedback, also can you rebase it
> > > We forked the webpack plugin and we are going to add this at scale for our 1000+ microfront-ends (or nano-frontends) to be exact.
> > 
> > 
> > Do you have any other changes in our code expect this? Because I think we can move them to our code (and you no need to use fork anymore or reduce time for maintenancing)
> 
> Here are the full list of changes:
> 
> 1. Add a mechanism to control which versions get shared (i.e registered for sharing as well as consumed from the share scope). The current PR covers this.
> 2. Do not load fallbacks if the version range that is requested e.g `^4.5.0` is satisfied by a higher version of the fallback as compared to a "loaded" version of the shared module in the runtime. For e.g if `foo` is already loaded at `4.10.0`, and the fallback version is`4.20.0`, **do not** load the fallback. MF 1.5 and MF 2.0 do not have this problem, but we pay a different cost w.r.t the remoteEntry size.
> 3. Add a way to deduplicate modules with the same versions. It is very common to have the same package + same version at different levels under `node_modules`. MF 1.0 in its current form registers all of these separately, even though the modules are not tree-shaken and therefore effectively the same. This greatly reduces the registration of shared modules that are heavily consumed by other shared modules (e.g themes, icons). It also reduces the build time because fewer fallbacks are built.
> 4. Ability to build fallbacks only for specific versions and modules. In a ultra large MFE world, we have discovered that having each MFE bring its own copy of a widely shared module is bad for performance, because each MFE author has no idea what another MFE author is going to use. Instead, we ended up creating specialized MFEs that act as the providers of these dependencies, and other MFEs simply consume them. The challenge is that these provider MFEs are typically only consumable by the same major version. e.g if the provider MFE supplies `foo@5.2.0`, and the consumer MFE requires `foo@5.1.0` and `foo@2.1.0` (transitive dependency), we want the `foo@5.1.0` to be shared/consumed, and `foo@2.1.0` to be bundled. Allowing `foo@2.1.0` to be bundled allows us to safely do a "per-module" fallback build. Think of it as a per module `import: false` depending on some condition. Again, this greatly reduces the amount of fallbacks + runtime inconsistencies associated with standard federation sharing.
> 
> If these changes are acceptable, I am okay with rebasing and sending an updated PR. Let me know. It is sad that we have zero control of the resolution mechanism used by MF 1.5/2.0 whereas all of the focus is on customizing the share behavior via runtimePlugins. runtimePlugins can achieve most of what I have outlined, but we still pay the cost of the runtime plugin + remote entry + fallback and what not.

for reference, the v2 runtime adds only 11kb (compressed) of additional payload. if you use externalRuntime https://module-federation.io/configure/experiments.html#externalruntime then the entire remoteEntry file (without compression) is 14kb - which is roughly the same size as v1 & the remoteEntry will use the hosts version of the v2 runtime. We have since improved the payload size further so that virtually no additional runtime code is added when external runtime is used.
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body:
Okay reading over the PR - i dont see any particular issue with the implementation, other than this will be the start of API deviations between v1 and v2 - we have guaranteed backward compatibility with webpack till now, users can expect the same with rspack as well - which still provides the v1 implementation as well in Rust. So in the future, I may drop v1 from rspack.

Given v1 constraints (no runtime plugins to control resolveShare) this seems acceptable. Though if you are going to support compile time filtration, instead of static options - having something like exclude(data)=>boolean or filter(data)=>boolean.

as you called out, this should not work with singleton.

I was looking at adding a filter callback for scenarios like "@mycompany/" where the trailing slash would aggressively share all things matching the prefix which internally leads to tens of thousands of packages getting added to shared - so a way to scope down what actually gets shared when doing prefix matching could be controlled in my 100GB+ monorepos. Providing a callback instead would mean less continued modifications to bundler if some other user needs it to work in some other way etc. For instance a callback would let you use your own semver rules if needed for things like beta packages or say canary patterns where builtin semver 

This may present problems in the future if webpack were to support v2's parallel share scopes and layers support (just speculation) 

I dont have any concerns related to v1 with this PR
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body: @ScriptedAlchemy Is it possible to backport this? I would like to avoid compatibility issues
author: alexander-akait

body:
Backport in which direction? 
This feature into v2? 

I'm not sure. I will need to look closer. Since our sharing and semver doesn't perform the satisfies calls at compile time. 

I do think there's some ramifications tho that we have actively sought to avoid when we were addressing the same issue in v2. 

We also support parallel share scope initialization. So init_sharing("default"|[array]) and shared modules can set issuerLayer and "layer," which makes cross layer sharing and semver matching more intricate. 🤔 

The problem the user is solving doesn't exist in v2 since we resolve this with runtime plugin and also provide 2 share algos version-first and loaded-first, our algos can also execute on edge worker so we can calculate the lock file without running the application - this is how our deploys function. So implementing it is redundant as the v2  has 30+ runtime hooks where they can already control every aspect of federation as they wish. During the running app, in the context of the issuer and for each import in. What the user is editing here is modification to loaded-first to try and make it behave like runtime plugin and version-first. 

In v2, we are not constrained by the singleton limitations that exclude introduces here. Since runtime plugin can allow users to break rules of singleton and bind singleton references across scopes per import, issuer, and layer. 

Since opening this PR, we have reduced our payload size by an additional 3x when external runtime is used. So, the remote entry size difference between v1 and v2 is about the same. 

So I think my stance on the subject is different. I'd just tell the user to use v2 and work with the user to bring down our payload size to an acceptable level, which we already did in response to another user who had DMd me saying "its better but not small enough" and a week or two later it was 3x smaller in external runtime mode - if I refactor some of the core I am aware of an additional 50% size reduction opportunity. 

Federation has always had requests for various customizations. Tobias has traditionally been against inflating the plugin interface complexity. This is something I have avoided, as well as more options in the plugin, which increases the risk and complexity of something that general users don't understand well. At my company, I can see users trying to use this option and end up breaking environments since we have over 10000 remotes, and some remotes have over 3000 versions. 

This would not scale in our usage where right now we theres over 30 million versions under my management. With the introduction of layers and parallel share scopes. If you had 2 layers and parallel scopes, that is now 90 million variants under management that can be combined  in any way and in use for months to years.

I'm also not sure how this will function with my plans for distributed treeshaking of shared modules, and JIT treeshaking at edge - both are already very complex. 

Exclude version is destructive operation. Over time, using import false like this means permutations can stop working. If the suppliers of those versions begin deploying and you have someone on other versions they can lose accesses to the share and are i capable of recovering since you cannot search the "network" of modules and locate a peer who can still supply it regardless of how old the variants are. Which is why we went the runtime plugin route instead so that these aspects can be managed within the hierarchy and context of the relationship between the peers and the system is able to maintain resilience at a enormous scale. 

Granted, we have the largest fleet of federation in the world. Nobody else is likely to have problems of this magnitude.
But because this is our daily operations, it's a valid concern. 

Given the constraints of v1 - it's a viable solution, but it's a problem that ultimately should be dealt with at runtime, not compile time, imo. 

Porting to v2 also means we have to update rolldown, rspack, webpack, rollup, vite implementations who all centrally depend on v2. Which is also why we refrain from alterations to the compiler plugin side where possible. 

Webpacks' internal implementation is the outlier. Which is why I do not have any opposition to the PR - it's a problem specific to v1 and users of v1 do not have a good solution to it. We are also a little more lenient around payload size, while still important and being addressed, it's been acceptable tradeoff in the near term to allow 11kb overhead to manage 30 million variants. Most v2 users have worked with us and accepted some short term size since they know we are working to address it safely. 

But if payload size is the primary concern above all else, v1 is lighter and i do understand the the need for this PR. That said, something like distributed tree shaking would eventually yield a smaller payload size since shared modules introduce a much larger impact in many cases. With external runtime + tree shake shared it would produce a smaller total payload. But again it takes time to produce that capability so v1 in near term may produce a smaller artifact.
author: ScriptedAlchemy
